content.text: What Is Zep?

Zep is a memory service for AI assistants that stores conversation history, embeds messages for semantic search, extracts facts and entity relationships into a temporal knowledge graph, and maintains long-term user profiles across sessions. Unlike framework-level memory modules that live in the application process, Zep runs as a standalone server with its own Postgres and Neo4j backends. This makes memory persistent across restarts, searchable, and shareable across multiple agent processes.
Setup and Session Management

Zep organises memory into users and sessions. A user_id represents a person or agent identity across all conversations. A session_id represents one conversation thread. Create the user and session before adding messages — attempting to write to a session that does not exist returns a 404.

# Install: pip install zep-python
# Start Zep server: docker-compose up (see github.com/getzep/zep)
# Cloud: use Zep Cloud at api.getzep.com with an API key.

from zep_python import ZepClient
from zep_python.memory import Memory, Message, Session

# Connect to a local Zep server
client = ZepClient(base_url="http://localhost:8000")

# Or use Zep Cloud:
# client = ZepClient(api_key="your-zep-api-key")

# Create a user and a session (session = one conversation)
client.user.add(user_id="u_42")

session_id = "sess_abc123"
client.memory.add_session(
    Session(session_id=session_id, user_id="u_42")
)
print(f"Session created: {session_id}")
Adding Messages and Retrieving Memory

Pass a Memory object containing a list of Message objects to memory.add(). Zep persists the messages, embeds them in the background, and after a short delay generates a rolling summary of older history. Calling memory.get() returns both the compressed summary and the most recent verbatim messages — ready to inject into an LLM prompt without any extra formatting.

# Add messages to a Zep session. Zep persists them, embeds them for semantic
# search, extracts entities, and automatically summarises older history.

from zep_python.memory import Memory, Message

messages = [
    Message(role="user",      role_type="user",      content="Our API uses FastAPI on Python 3.12."),
    Message(role="assistant", role_type="assistant",  content="Understood. I will keep that in mind."),
    Message(role="user",      role_type="user",       content="The Postgres version is 16 and we are using pgvector."),
    Message(role="assistant", role_type="assistant",  content="Got it — FastAPI on Python 3.12, Postgres 16 with pgvector."),
]

client.memory.add(
    session_id=session_id,
    memory=Memory(messages=messages),
)

# Retrieve memory: Zep returns a summary of older turns + recent verbatim messages
mem = client.memory.get(session_id=session_id)

print("Summary:", mem.summary.content if mem.summary else "(none yet)")
print("Recent messages:")
for m in mem.messages:
    print(f"  [{m.role}] {m.content}")
Semantic Search

memory.search() performs semantic search over a session's stored messages using the server-side embeddings. Pass search_scope='messages' to search raw turns or 'summary' to search the compressed summaries. Use search_type='mmr' (maximal marginal relevance) to diversify results when the top-k hits would otherwise be near-duplicates. For cross-session recall, search_sessions() searches all sessions belonging to a user.

# Semantic search over a session's stored messages.
# Zep searches the embedded message store and returns ranked results.

from zep_python.memory import MemorySearchPayload

payload = MemorySearchPayload(
    text="what database are we using?",
    search_scope="messages",   # or "summary"
    search_type="similarity",  # or "mmr" for maximal marginal relevance
)

results = client.memory.search(session_id=session_id, search_payload=payload, limit=5)

for r in results:
    print(f"score={r.score:.3f}  [{r.message.role}] {r.message.content}")

# Cross-session search: search all sessions for a user
user_results = client.memory.search_sessions(
    user_id="u_42",
    search_payload=MemorySearchPayload(text="postgres version", search_scope="messages"),
    limit=5,
)
for r in user_results:
    print(f"[session {r.session_id}] {r.message.content}")
User Facts and Long-Term Profile

Zep automatically extracts named facts from conversation messages — technology choices, preferences, deadlines, relationships — and stores them as user facts keyed by user_id. These facts persist across all sessions and are included when calling memory.get_context(), which assembles a ready-to-inject context string containing the session summary, recent messages, and relevant user facts. This gives the LLM long-term knowledge about the user without the agent storing anything manually.

# Zep automatically extracts facts from conversation messages and stores them
# as long-term user facts. Facts persist across sessions — they represent
# what the system knows about this user regardless of which session said it.

# Get all facts for a user (Zep populates these automatically)
user = client.user.get(user_id="u_42")
print("Extracted user facts:")
for fact in user.facts or []:
    print(f"  - {fact.fact}  (confidence={fact.rating})")

# You can also add facts manually
from zep_python.user import CreateUserRequest
client.user.update(
    user_id="u_42",
    user_data={"email": "alice@example.com", "timezone": "Europe/London"},
)

# Retrieve the memory context ready for injection into a prompt.
# This includes: summary, recent messages, AND relevant user facts.
context = client.memory.get_context(session_id=session_id)
print("\nContext for LLM injection:")
print(context)
LangChain Integration

ZepChatMessageHistory is a drop-in LangChain chat_memory backend. Pass it to any ConversationBufferMemory and the existing chain works unchanged — turns are written to Zep instead of in-process. Set memory_type='perpetual' to use Zep's full summary+facts context; set 'message_window' for a plain last-N sliding window without fact extraction.

# ZepChatMessageHistory is a drop-in replacement for LangChain's in-memory
# history — swap it in and your existing chain gets Zep persistence for free.

from langchain_community.chat_message_histories import ZepChatMessageHistory
from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory
from langchain.chains import ConversationChain
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI

ZEP_API_URL = "http://localhost:8000"
SESSION_ID  = "lc_sess_001"

history = ZepChatMessageHistory(
    session_id=SESSION_ID,
    url=ZEP_API_URL,
    memory_type="perpetual",   # "perpetual" = summary + facts; "message_window" = last-N
)

memory = ConversationBufferMemory(
    memory_key="chat_history",
    chat_memory=history,
    return_messages=True,
)

chain = ConversationChain(
    llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),
    memory=memory,
    verbose=False,
)

chain.predict(input="We decided to use Redis for session caching.")
reply = chain.predict(input="What did we decide about caching?")
print(reply)
# Zep persists both turns and will extract "Redis for session caching" as a fact.
Common Pitfalls
Summary lag

— Zep generates a session summary asynchronously after messages are added. Calling get_memory() immediately after add() may return an empty summary — wait at least one second or poll until mem.summary is non-None in development.
Fact extraction is eventually consistent
— Entity and user facts are extracted by a background job. Do not treat them as immediately available after a single add() call — they appear within a few seconds depending on server load.
session_id must be pre-created
— Calling memory.add() with a session_id that does not exist raises a 404. Always call memory.add_session() before adding messages to a new session.
Cross-session search scope
— search() only searches the specified session. To search across all of a user's sessions, use search_sessions() with the user_id. Mixing the two is a common source of missing results.
Cloud vs self-hosted API differences

— Zep Cloud (api.getzep.com) enables fact extraction and knowledge graphs by default. The self-hosted open-source edition requires a paid license for these features — check the plan before relying on user.facts in production.
Knowledge Graph and Fact Extraction

Zep runs a background pipeline that parses each new batch of messages, extracts named entities and relationships, and writes them to a temporal knowledge graph (Neo4j). Each edge records a timestamp, enabling queries such as 'what did Alice approve last Tuesday?' The extracted facts are also surfaced as structured user facts on the User object. Graph-backed fact extraction is available in Zep Cloud and in self-hosted Pro tier.

# Zep extracts entities and relationships into a temporal knowledge graph.
# The graph is queryable via the memory.search() 'graph' scope or the
# graph API. Edges carry a timestamp, so you can see how facts changed.

from zep_python.memory import MemorySearchPayload

# First, load some conversation turns with entity mentions
from zep_python.memory import Memory, Message

client.memory.add(
    session_id=session_id,
    memory=Memory(messages=[
        Message(role='user', role_type='user',
                content='Alice is the tech lead. Bob reports to Alice.'),
        Message(role='user', role_type='user',
                content='Alice approved the migration to Postgres 16 last Tuesday.'),
    ]),
)

import time; time.sleep(2)  # wait for background extraction

# Search the graph for relationships involving Alice
graph_results = client.memory.search(
    session_id=session_id,
    search_payload=MemorySearchPayload(
        text='who does Bob report to?',
        search_scope='summary',   # summary includes graph-extracted facts
    ),
    limit=5,
)
for r in graph_results:
    print(f'score={r.score:.3f}  {r.summary.content[:120]}')

# Get all extracted facts for the user (populated from all sessions)
user = client.user.get(user_id='u_42')
for fact in user.facts or []:
    print(f'[{fact.rating:.2f}] {fact.fact}')
Exporting, Deleting, and Data Lifecycle

Zep provides a full CRUD API for session data. Use get_session_messages() with pagination to export a complete conversation history for audit logs or fine-tuning datasets. memory.delete() removes a session and all its messages; user.delete() removes a user and every session and fact associated with them. These are the correct hooks for GDPR right-to-erasure requests.

# Export a session's full history as a list of Message objects.
# Useful for offline analysis, fine-tuning datasets, or migrating to a
# different memory backend.

# Paginate through all messages (Zep returns them newest-first by default)
all_messages = []
page = 0
page_size = 50
while True:
    chunk = client.memory.get_session_messages(
        session_id=session_id,
        limit=page_size,
        cursor=page * page_size,
    )
    if not chunk:
        break
    all_messages.extend(chunk)
    if len(chunk) < page_size:
        break
    page += 1

print(f'Total messages exported: {len(all_messages)}')
for m in all_messages[:5]:
    print(f'[{m.role}] {m.content[:80]}')

# Delete a session and all its messages (GDPR / data removal)
# client.memory.delete(session_id=session_id)

# Delete a user and ALL their sessions and facts
# client.user.delete(user_id='u_42')
Token-Budget-Aware Context Injection

memory.get_context() returns a pre-formatted string that blends summary, recent messages, and user facts. Measure its token length before including it in a prompt and truncate from the front (oldest content) if it exceeds the budget. This keeps the most recent and most factual content while staying within the model's context limit.

# Assemble a token-budget-aware context string for injection into the prompt.
# get_context() returns a pre-formatted string; trim it if needed.

import tiktoken

CONTEXT_TOKEN_BUDGET = 1500
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o-mini')

raw_context = client.memory.get_context(session_id=session_id)

tokens = enc.encode(raw_context)
if len(tokens) > CONTEXT_TOKEN_BUDGET:
    # Truncate from the front (oldest content) to stay within budget
    tokens = tokens[-CONTEXT_TOKEN_BUDGET:]
    raw_context = enc.decode(tokens)

system_prompt = (
    'You are a helpful assistant. Use the memory context below when relevant.\n\n'
    f'[Memory Context]\n{raw_context}\n[/Memory Context]'
)
print(f'System prompt tokens: {len(enc.encode(system_prompt))}')
print(system_prompt[:400])
Summary
Zep is a standalone memory server — persistent across restarts, shareable across processes, and backed by Postgres and Neo4j.
Organise memory by user_id (cross-session profile) and session_id (single conversation); always create both before adding messages.
memory.get() returns a combined context of rolling summary + recent verbatim messages; memory.get_context() also appends extracted user facts.
Semantic search works within a session (search()) or across all sessions for a user (search_sessions()); MMR mode reduces near-duplicate hits.
ZepChatMessageHistory integrates Zep into any LangChain chain as a zero-code-change persistence backend.
Fact extraction and knowledge graphs are asynchronous — do not read user.facts immediately after add(); allow a few seconds for the background pipeline to complete.
